source.zh: 大卫·库西克《六族古史概略》（1827）；J·N·B·休伊特《易洛魁宇宙论》（美国民族学局年报第21辑，1903；第43辑，1928）；阿瑟·C·帕克《塞尼卡神话与民间故事》（1923）；詹姆斯·R·沃克《拉科塔信仰与仪式》（德马利、雅纳编，1980）与《拉科塔神话》（雅纳编，1983）；约瑟夫·艾普斯·布朗《圣烟斗：奥格拉拉苏族的七大圣礼》（1953，据黑麋鹿口述）；华盛顿·马修斯《纳瓦霍传说》（1897）；艾琳·奥布莱恩《迪内：纳瓦霍人的起源神话》（1956，据桑多瓦尔口述）；保罗·G·祖尔布罗德《迪内·巴哈内：纳瓦霍创世故事》（1984）
source.en: David Cusick, Sketches of Ancient History of the Six Nations (1827); J. N. B. Hewitt, Iroquoian Cosmology, First Part (21st Annual Report of the Bureau of American Ethnology, 1903) and Second Part (43rd Annual Report, 1928); Arthur C. Parker, Seneca Myths and Folk Tales (1923); James R. Walker, Lakota Belief and Ritual (ed. DeMallie and Jahner, 1980) and Lakota Myth (ed. Jahner, 1983); Joseph Epes Brown, The Sacred Pipe: Black Elk's Account of the Seven Rites of the Oglala Sioux (1953); Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navaho Indians (1956, from the account of Sandoval); Paul G. Zolbrod, Diné bahané: The Navajo Creation Story (1984)

note.zh:
「北美原住民」不是一个神话体系。仅美国联邦承认的部落就有五百多个，加拿大另有六百多个第一民族社区，语言分属十几个互不相干的语系，开端的讲法也各是各的：有从水底捞土的，有从地下爬出来的，有乌鸦把日光从匣子里放出来的，也有民族并不把开端当作可以随口讲给外人的事。本页只取三个记录相对充分的例子——豪德诺硕尼（易洛魁联盟，主要依塞尼卡与奥农达加的记录）、拉科塔、迪内（纳瓦霍）。这三套叙事彼此独立，不是同一条时间线的三段；下面按顺序排列只是版面需要，不表示先后，也不表示这三家比别家更有代表性。族名尽量用自称：易洛魁、苏、纳瓦霍都是外来称呼。三者都是活着的信仰，本页只用已刊布的公开材料，不涉及仪轨与传承中的知识。

版本问题一条条说。天女下坠：库西克1827年的《六族古史概略》是原住民自己用英文写下的最早文本，但他动笔时已置身基督教与英文史书的环境中；休伊特1903与1928年刊出的莫霍克、奥农达加、塞尼卡三系记录彼此不同——天女是失足、被推下、还是自己下来的，取土成功的是麝鼠还是蟾蜍，双生子叫什么名字、用什么兵器决胜，各版都有出入。拉科塔的地下起源：今天读到的那个连贯版本主要出自詹姆斯·沃克，他1896至1914年在松岭保留地当医生，从乔治·剑（George Sword）等多位圣者处收得片段，再编成一部完整的「循环」；1983年整理出版时，编者伊莱恩·雅纳明确指出其中含有沃克本人的文学编排，不能当作单一定本。拉科塔另有一套把星空与大地对起来的知识：黑山的地形与星座一一相配，仪式的季节路线随日头在两者之间走，见罗纳德·古德曼《拉科塔星辰知识》（1992）。把出世之处指认为黑山的风洞，在今天的拉科塔口传与黑山的土地主张中分量很重，但并非所有记录都这么讲。

《迪内·巴哈内》的世界层数本身就没有定论：多数讲法上到第四世界（闪光的世界），也有记录算作第五层。第一男与第一女在哪一层生成，各家也不一致——马修斯1897年的记录把玉米穗化生二人放在上来之后的世界里，奥布莱恩1956年与祖尔布罗德1984年的版本放在最下面的黑世界。迪内传统认为这些故事只在冬天讲。三套叙事里都没有「天地分离」这一步：豪德诺硕尼的天与水从一开始就是两层，拉科塔与迪内的世界是上下叠着的多层，没有谁需要把天从地上劈开、撑起。西南部的霍皮与其他普韦布洛民族另有自己的出世叙事，与迪内的不是一回事，本页不并入。
note.en:
There is no such thing as a single Native American mythology. More than five hundred tribes are federally recognized in the United States and more than six hundred First Nations communities exist in Canada, speaking languages that belong to more than a dozen unrelated families, and their accounts of how things began differ accordingly: earth brought up from under water, people climbing out of the ground, Raven letting the daylight out of the box that held it — and, in some nations, a beginning that is simply not told to outsiders on request. Three reasonably well documented accounts are set out here: the Haudenosaunee (the Iroquois Confederacy, chiefly the Seneca and Onondaga records), the Lakota, and the Diné (Navajo). They are three separate traditions, not three parts of one story; the order below is a matter of layout only and implies no sequence and no claim that these three stand for the rest. Where a nation has its own name for itself, that name is used: Iroquois, Sioux and Navajo are all names given from outside. All three are living traditions. Only published material is drawn on here, and nothing that belongs to ceremony or to initiated knowledge.

On the versions, one by one. Sky Woman: Cusick's Sketches (1827) is the earliest account written in English by a Haudenosaunee man, but he wrote it inside a world of missionaries and English history books. The Mohawk, Onondaga and Seneca texts Hewitt published in 1903 and 1928 do not agree with one another — whether Sky Woman fell, was pushed, or climbed down; whether it was the muskrat or the toad that came up with the mud; what the twins are called and what weapon settles their contest. The Lakota emergence: the connected version people read today comes mainly from James R. Walker, a physician at Pine Ridge from 1896 to 1914, who collected fragments from George Sword and other holy men and assembled them into a single cycle. When Elaine Jahner edited that material for publication in 1983 she said plainly that Walker's own literary arrangement is part of what we have, and that it cannot be treated as a fixed canonical text. A separate body of Lakota knowledge sets the terrain of the Black Hills against the constellations, site answering to star, with the ceremonial year following the sun between the two (Ronald Goodman, Lakota Star Knowledge, 1992). Identifying Wind Cave as the place of emergence carries great weight in Lakota oral tradition today and in the Black Hills land claim, but not every record says so.

Diné bahané does not settle even the number of worlds: most tellings bring the people up into a fourth world, the Glittering World, while some records count five. Nor do the records agree on where First Man and First Woman came into being — Matthews (1897) has the ears of corn become them in the world reached after the ascent, while O'Bryan (1956) and Zolbrod (1984) place it in the lowest, Black World. Diné tradition holds that these stories are told in winter. None of the three accounts contains a separation of sky and earth: for the Haudenosaunee, sky and water are two realms from the outset, and the Lakota and Diné worlds are stacked one above another, so nothing has to be split apart or propped up. The Hopi and other Pueblo peoples of the Southwest have emergence accounts of their own, which are not the same as the Diné one and are not folded in here.

title.en: A world in the sky, water underneath

text.zh: 豪德诺硕尼（易洛魁联盟）的讲法从上面开始。天上有一个世界，那里有人、有村落，中央长着一棵大树，开发光的花；没有太阳，光就出自这棵树。下面没有一寸干土，只有一片无边的水，水禽浮在水面，会潜水的兽在水里游。天上世界的首领病倒了，有人说要把那棵大树连根挖出来治病。树一动，根底下就裂开一个洞，洞口正对着下面的水。
text.en: The Haudenosaunee account begins overhead. There was a world in the sky, with people and villages in it and a great tree at its center that bore blossoms giving light; there was no sun, and the light came from the tree. Below, there was not a handful of dry ground anywhere — only water without a shore, with waterfowl floating on it and diving animals underneath. When the chief of the sky world fell ill, it was said the tree should be pulled up to cure him. The tree came away, and where its roots had been there was a hole, and under the hole lay the water.

title.zh: 麝鼠把一撮泥堆在龟背上
title.en: Muskrat's handful of mud on the turtle's back

text.zh: 天女从洞里坠下去——有的讲法说她失足，有的说是被推的，也有的说她自己下来的；坠落时她抓住洞口的草木，手里攥着种子和烟草。水禽（多说是雁或天鹅）飞上去用翅膀接住她，把她放在一只大龟的背上。会潜水的兽轮番下水取土：水獭、河狸、潜鸟一个个沉了下去，最后麝鼠浮上来，爪子里攥着一撮泥（塞尼卡的一些讲法里是蟾蜍从水底衔上来的）。泥抹在龟甲上，天女在上面绕着圈走，土越走越宽，长成大地。五大湖的阿尼什纳贝人讲的是同一个母题的另一头：灭世洪水之后，纳纳博佐攀着浮木派动物下潜，麝鼠拿命换回那撮泥。北美不少民族至今把这块大陆叫作「龟岛」。
text.en: Sky Woman went down through the hole — she slipped, or she was pushed, or she climbed down of her own will, depending on who is telling it — and as she fell she snatched at the plants around the rim and came away holding seeds and tobacco. The waterfowl, usually geese or swans, flew up and took her on their wings and set her on the back of a great turtle. The diving animals went down for earth in turn: otter, beaver and loon went under and did not come back, and at last the muskrat surfaced with a smear of mud in its paw. Some Seneca tellings give the find to the toad instead. The mud was spread on the turtle's shell, and Sky Woman walked around on it in a circle, and the ground widened as she walked until it was the earth. Among the Anishinaabe of the Great Lakes the same motif runs the other way round: after a flood has drowned the world, Nanabozho clings to a floating log and sends the animals down, and the muskrat dies bringing up the mud. Many nations still call this continent Turtle Island.

title.en: Clay for people, an antler for the fight

text.zh: 天女在龟背上生下一个女儿。女儿长大后怀了身孕，难产而死，留下一对双生子：一个照常出生，另一个从她的侧身破出来，是燧石（塔维斯卡隆）。天女埋了女儿，坟头长出玉米、豆子和南瓜——豪德诺硕尼人称作「三姊妹」的三样作物，还长出了烟草。兄弟俩各干各的：善的一方（塞尼卡称萨普林，又称「撑天者」）用泥捏出人，让河水两边对流，划船上下都不必费力，把果子结得好摘；燧石跟在后面改：河只许一个方向流，堆起急流与乱石，给鱼身上安上刺，往林子里放怪物。最后两人动手较量，多数讲法里善的一方用鹿角把燧石打倒，燧石退到人居的地面之外，碎在地上的成了燧石块。他留下的东西一样没撤走：荆棘、急流、蚊虫、寒冬，都还在。
text.en: On the turtle's back Sky Woman bore a daughter. The daughter grew, conceived, and died in childbirth, leaving twins: one born in the ordinary way, the other, Flint, breaking out through her side. Sky Woman buried her, and out of the grave came corn, beans and squash — the three crops the Haudenosaunee call the Three Sisters — and tobacco. The brothers then worked against each other. The good one, called Sapling by the Seneca and also Holder of the Heavens, molded people out of clay, set the rivers running both ways at once so that a canoe need never be paddled upstream, and made fruit easy to reach. Flint came behind him and changed it: the rivers were made to run one way only, rapids and boulders were piled into them, bones were put into fish, and monsters were let loose in the woods. In the end the two fought it out, and in most tellings Sapling struck Flint down with a deer antler; Flint was driven off the inhabited ground, and the pieces that broke from him are the flint lying about in the fields. Nothing he made was taken back. The thorns, the rapids, the mosquitoes and the winter all stayed.

title.zh: 从风洞出来的人，跟出来的野牛
title.en: Out of Wind Cave, and the buffalo behind them

text.zh: 拉科塔的讲法从地下开始。族人（普特·奥亚特，野牛之民）住在地面之下。蜘蛛骗子伊克托米与双面女阿农·伊特合谋，拿烤肉和鞣好的皮衣给他们看，说上面的世界样样都有；托卡黑（意为「第一个」）信了，领着一部分族人顺着一个洞爬到地面上。上来才知道地上要挨饿受冻，路也回不去了。有的讲法说，留在下面的族人后来化作野牛，从同一个洞里出来，把肉、皮、骨和筋交给地上的人，拉科塔人由此把野牛当作亲族。今天的口传把那个出口指认在黑山的风洞（Washun Niya，「呼吸的洞」）：天然洞口不过一尺见方，人根本钻不进去，气流却终日在里面进出，像大地在喘气。
text.en: The Lakota account begins underground. The people — the Pte Oyate, the Buffalo Nation — lived beneath the surface. Iktomi the spider trickster and Anog Ite, the Double Face Woman, worked out a scheme between them: they showed the people roasted meat and clothes of dressed hide and told them the world above had everything in it. Tokahe, whose name means the first one, believed them and led a party up through a cave to the surface, where they found hunger and cold waiting and no way back down. In some tellings the people who stayed below afterwards became buffalo and came up through the same hole, giving their meat, hides, bone and sinew to those already on the surface, which is why the Lakota count the buffalo as kin. Oral tradition today puts the opening at Wind Cave in the Black Hills — Washun Niya, the breathing hole — where the natural entrance is a gap barely a foot across, far too small to crawl through, with the air moving in and out of it all day as though the earth were breathing.

title.zh: 白野牛犊女带来查努帕
title.en: White Buffalo Calf Woman brings the pipe

text.zh: 饥荒的年头，两名斥候出去找野牛群，迎面遇上一位穿白鹿皮衣的女子。一人起了淫念上前，云雾罩住他，散开时地上只剩一堆白骨；另一人回营报信，族人搭起圣帐候着。她进营住了四天，把查努帕（红石斗钵、木斗柄）交到首领「立空角」手里，讲清怎样用它祈祷，并说还有七大圣礼要随这支烟斗而来——汗屋净礼、求视、太阳舞都在其中（布朗记下的黑麋鹿口述里，七礼是此后一件件显现的）。她走出营地，在草地上翻滚，每翻一次换一次颜色，最后成了一头白色野牛犊，走了。那支烟斗至今由南达科他夏延河保留地绿草社区的「望马」（Looking Horse）家族世代看守，现任守斗者被认作第十九代。
text.en: In a year of famine two scouts went out to look for buffalo and met a woman in white buckskin. One of them approached her with lust in his mind; a cloud came down over him, and when it lifted there were only bones on the ground. The other went back to camp with word of her, and the people put up a lodge and waited. She stayed four days. She placed the chanunpa — a bowl of red pipestone, a wooden stem — in the hands of the chief, Standing Hollow Horn, showed the people how to pray with it, and told them that seven rites would come with the pipe, among them the sweat lodge, the vision quest and the sun dance. In Black Elk's account as Brown set it down, those rites were disclosed one at a time afterwards. Then she walked out of the camp, rolled on the grass, changed color at every turn, and went off across the plain as a white buffalo calf. The pipe is still kept by the Looking Horse family at Green Grass on the Cheyenne River Reservation in South Dakota, where the present keeper is held to be the nineteenth in the line.

title.zh: 黑世界里的第一男与第一女
title.en: First Man and First Woman in the Black World

text.zh: 迪内（纳瓦霍人的自称）的创世叙事《迪内·巴哈内》讲的是「出现」：人从底下的世界一层层爬上来，末了才落在这一层。最底下是黑世界，只有四个角，四边立着黑、白、蓝、黄四色的云；住在里面的是「气之人」（Nilchʼi dineʼé，一译昆虫人）和圣人（Diyin Dineʼé）。祖尔布罗德整理的版本里，黑云与白云相接处生出第一男（Áłtsé Hastiin），蓝云与黄云相接处生出第一女（Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́），两人身边各有一穗玉米，一白一黄。黑世界的居民因争斗与不轨被逐，只得往上走：蓝世界、黄世界，一层接一层，每一层都待不长。
text.en: The Diné account, Diné bahané, is an emergence: the people climbed up into this world through the worlds stacked beneath it, one after another. At the bottom lies the Black World, a place with four corners and nothing else, columns of cloud standing at its edges — black, white, blue, yellow. Its inhabitants are the Air-Spirit People (Nilchʼi dineʼé, sometimes rendered as insect people) and the Holy People, the Diyin Dineʼé. In the version Zolbrod set down, First Man, Áłtsé Hastiin, came into being where the black cloud met the white one, and First Woman, Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́, where the blue met the yellow, each with an ear of corn beside them, his white and hers yellow. Quarreling and misconduct got the inhabitants driven out, so they went up: into the Blue World, then the Yellow World, never able to stay long in either.

phase.en: Diné: the flood and the climb

title.en: Coyote takes the water monster's children

text.zh: 在黄世界，男人和女人闹翻了，分居大河两岸各过各的，两边都过不下去，才又合到一处。后来郊狼（马伊）从水怪蒂霍尔措迪（Tééhoołtsódii）家里抱走两个孩子，藏进自己的皮袄。水从四面追着涨上来。众人种下一根芦苇，芦苇长到天顶，人和动物顺着中空的茎往上爬。上去之后有人发现郊狼怀里的孩子，把孩子送还下去，水才退。从这个洞口出来的世界，多数讲法称第四世界，即「闪光的世界」，也有记录把它算作第五层。
text.en: In the Yellow World the men and the women fell out and separated, living on opposite banks of a river, and neither side could manage alone; in the end they came back together. Later Coyote, Mąʼii, took two children from the house of Tééhoołtsódii, the water monster, and hid them inside his coat. The water rose after him from every side. The people planted a reed, and it grew until it touched the sky, and they climbed up the hollow stem, people and animals together. Once they were up, someone found the children in Coyote's coat and sent them back down, and only then did the water go down with them. The world they came out into is called the fourth in most tellings, the Glittering World, though some records count it as the fifth.

phase.en: Diné: the world as it stands

title.zh: 四座圣山、乱撒的星、变化女
title.en: Four mountains, a fistful of stars, Changing Woman

text.zh: 第一男与第一女带着从下面世界取来的土，堆出四座圣山，把迪内的家园围在当中：东边白贝山（布兰卡峰）、南边绿松石山（泰勒山）、西边鲍贝山（旧金山峰）、北边黑玉山（赫斯佩勒斯峰），分别用闪电、燧石刀、日光和彩虹钉在地上。第一男按次序往夜空里一颗颗摆星（也有讲法把摆星的活儿归给黑神哈什切什津尼），郊狼嫌慢，抓起剩下的一把撒了出去，成了银河与满天乱星。后来第一男在乔奥利伊山（Gobernador Knob）上拾到一个女婴，就是变化女（Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé）：她四天长成，与太阳生下英雄双子——怪物杀手（Naayééʼ Neizghání）与生于水者（Tóbájíshchíní）。双子上太阳的家里讨来闪电做的武器，回来清除世上的怪物；泰勒山脚下那片黑熔岩，传统上说是巨怪耶伊措凝住的血。变化女搓下自己身上的皮，造出最初的四个氏族——塔屋、苦水、泥、绕行——迪内人报家门，至今先报母亲的氏族。
text.en: First Man and First Woman had carried up soil from the worlds below, and with it they built four mountains around the Diné homeland: Blanca Peak in the east, dressed in white shell; Mount Taylor in the south, in turquoise; the San Francisco Peaks in the west, in abalone; Hesperus Peak in the north, in jet. Each was pinned to the ground — with lightning, a flint knife, a sunbeam, a rainbow. First Man then began setting the stars into the night sky one by one, in order — other tellings give the work to Black God, Haashchʼééshzhiní — until Coyote lost patience, grabbed what was left and flung it up in a fistful; that is the Milky Way and the scattered stars. Later First Man found a baby girl on Chʼóolʼį́ʼí, Gobernador Knob: Changing Woman, Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé, who grew to womanhood in four days and bore the Hero Twins to the Sun — Monster Slayer, Naayééʼ Neizghání, and Born for Water, Tóbájíshchíní. The twins went to their father's house, got weapons of lightning from him, and cleared the monsters off the earth; the black lava spread below Mount Taylor is said to be the congealed blood of the giant Yéʼiitsoh. Changing Woman rubbed skin from her own body and made the first four clans — Towering House, Bitter Water, Mud, One-Walks-Around — and a Diné person still gives the mother's clan first when saying who they are.
